basic_info.name: Anti-Graffiti Coating Design
basic_info.name_ja: 防落書きコーティング設計
basic_info.description: Anti-graffiti coatings are designed to maximize HSP mismatch between the protective topcoat and common graffiti materials (spray paints, markers, inks). By ensuring a large HSP distance (high Ra or RED > 1) between the coating surface and graffiti media, the graffiti cannot wet, penetrate, or bond strongly to the surface, enabling easy removal with mild solvents or pressure washing without damaging the underlying coating.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: The principle is inverted compared to adhesion optimization: the goal is to maximize incompatibility between the topcoat and graffiti materials. A coating with HSP far from common graffiti solvents and binders (typically aromatic hydrocarbons, ketones, and alkyd/acrylic resins) will resist penetration and adhesion of graffiti. Fluoropolymers and silicone-based coatings achieve this naturally due to their very low dP and dH values, creating large Ra distances from most graffiti formulations. The coating must simultaneously maintain adhesion to the underlying substrate (low Ra with substrate) while repelling graffiti (high Ra with graffiti).
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra_graffiti = sqrt(4*(dD_coat - dD_graffiti)^2 + (dP_coat - dP_graffiti)^2 + (dH_coat - dH_graffiti)^2); RED_graffiti = Ra_graffiti / R0_coat >> 1 desired
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED > 1.5 for effective graffiti resistance; Ra > 8 MPa^0.5 preferred between coating and graffiti binder

required_inputs: HSP of common graffiti materials (spray paint binders, marker solvents, permanent ink resins)
data_sources: HSPiP database for fluoropolymers, silicones, polyurethanes
Literature HSP for common spray paint formulations (alkyd, acrylic, lacquer)
Manufacturer data for anti-graffiti coating resins
Contact angle measurements for surface characterization
Group contribution methods for novel coating chemistries
complementary_parameters: Surface energy and contact angle (water and diiodomethane)

limitations: Graffiti materials vary widely in composition; a single HSP target for 'graffiti' is an oversimplification
Surface texture and porosity effects are not captured by HSP alone
Sacrificial anti-graffiti systems work by a different mechanism (removal of coating layer) not well described by HSP mismatch
Weathering and UV degradation change coating surface HSP over time
Permanent anti-graffiti coatings may lose performance after repeated cleaning cycles
known_failure_modes: Aggressive solvent-based graffiti (e.g., bitumen markers) that can penetrate even low-energy surfaces
Porous substrates (bare concrete, brick) where graffiti penetrates below the coating
Coatings that degrade under UV exposure, increasing surface energy and graffiti adhesion over time
Etching-type vandalism (acid-based) that operates by chemical attack rather than adhesion
accuracy.validation_method: Graffiti application and removal testing per ASTM D6578 or EN 1062-11, contact angle measurement before and after cleaning cycles, cross-cut adhesion testing of coating to substrate, accelerated weathering (QUV) followed by graffiti resistance re-testing

key_references: Hansen, C.M. 'Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook', 2nd ed., CRC Press, 2007
Rabea, A.M., Mohseni, M. 'Anti-graffiti coatings: a review', Journal of Coatings Technology and Research, 2015, Vol. 12, pp. 1-15
Lettieri, M., Masieri, M. 'Surface characterization and effectiveness of anti-graffiti coatings on cultural heritage stone materials', Applied Surface Science, 2014
Abbott, S. 'Chemical resistance and Hansen Solubility Parameters', HSPiP documentation, 2015
literature.regulatory_relevance: VOC content regulations (EPA, EU Directive 2004/42/EC) apply to anti-graffiti coating formulations. No specific regulatory framework references HSP-based design, but municipal and transportation authorities specify performance standards for anti-graffiti systems.

commercial_tools: Proprietary formulation tools from coating manufacturers (e.g., Teknos, Protectosil)
